name: General Motors
description: Designed human-centered experiences for AI-powered features, balancing cutting-edge technology with intuitive interfaces that prioritized safety and user trust. Identified and solved critical UX gaps, proposing innovative design patterns that bridged technical capabilities with real-world user needs. Collaborated with cross-functional teams (engineering, product, data science) to interpret user analytics and behavioral data, iterating designs that increased feature adoption by 40%. Drove process standardization initiatives that reduced design-to-development handoff time by 34% and improved team efficiency

name: National Sci-Fi Museum
description: I approached the problem by trying to understand the primary interactions users would have with a museum app. For true Sci-Fi fans like Dan, Harry, and Tay, this could become a favorite app, so it needed to be a catered experience. For visitors less interested in Sci-Fi like Lori, the app had to be accessible and avoid jarring flows or interaction methods. To build a crafted experience, I opted for an alternative typeface. I used Avenir Next for its futuristic feel over the more neutral SF Pro or the serif New York, which might have felt too old-fashioned or stuffy. Other Sci-Fi fonts like Orbitron were considered but ultimately rejected to ensure legibility for a diverse audience. Purple was chosen as the primary theme color to stand out from the common blue, red, yellow, and green in apps. It makes the app noticeable, recognizable, and connects with the mystery and unknown that is essential to the genre. I relied on many Apple-designed components for accessibility and familiarity, especially for users who might not use the app daily. These tried-and-true elements ensure usability isn't a barrier to enjoying the museum. One assumption I made was that a public museum would handle most accessibility needs. If not, I would have added more detail to activity and map views to show experiences were accessible. Lori's persona, and her son Daniel, made me consider icons denoting accessibility features to help users make the best use of their time. The most important accessibility aspect I followed was embracing iOS best practices, along with attention to color contrast and font legibility. Inclusivity was central to the app's design. Profiles and a community chat feature allow users to personalize their experience and feel part of the museum community. Tay's persona made me think about using iconography to denote media types in exhibits, helping users engage with content they truly care about.
features: Avenir Next-led visual system for a futuristic but legible UI
Accessibility-conscious interaction patterns and color contrast
Inclusive features such as icon-supported exhibit media discovery
Persona-driven design decisions for diverse museum audiences

name: Stellantis

Designed and validated new interaction patterns leveraging emerging technology paradigms, conducting usability testing that informed product roadmap decisions. Owned end-to-end design process from research through prototyping to implementation, ensuring cohesive experiences across web and mobile touchpoints. Collaborated globally with engineers to deliver pixel-perfect implementations, establishing design-development workflows that maintained quality at scale

embed.title: Google Assistant Redesign for the Blind presentation

name: Food Inspections Online Postings
description: MiSAFE is the Michigan Department of Agriculture and Rural Development's public-facing system for food establishment inspection reports — covering grocery stores, food processors, food warehouses, and convenience stores across the state. Inspection data is available from April 2022 forward and is searchable by establishment name, address, and city. I redeveloped the system to meet WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility requirements ahead of federal enforcement deadlines and aligned the experience with the One State, One Brand design initiative, ensuring the interface is consistent with the broader Michigan.gov ecosystem.

clients.name: Michigan Department of Agriculture, and Rural Development

features: WCAG 2.1 AA compliance for federal ADA enforcement deadline
Alignment with One State, One Brand visual and UX standards
Public search by establishment name, address, and city
Covers grocery stores, food processors, warehouses, and convenience stores

name: Well Record Retrieval System
description: The Well Record Retrieval System provides public access to over one million water well, pump, and abandoned well plugging records filed electronically in the Wellogic system since 2000. The data feeds into critical downstream systems including Michigan's Water Well Viewer, Drinking Water GIS, and the Water Withdrawal Assessment Tool. I redeveloped the public-facing retrieval interface to meet WCAG 2.1 AA requirements ahead of federal enforcement and aligned it with the One State, One Brand design standards to provide a consistent experience across Michigan.gov.

clients.name: Michigan Department of Enivronment, Great Lakes, and Energy

features: WCAG 2.1 AA compliance for federal ADA enforcement deadline
Alignment with One State, One Brand visual and UX standards
Public access to over one million well and pump records statewide
Supports downstream tools including Water Well Viewer and WWAT

name: Operator Training and Certification Information System (OTCIS / OpCert)
description: OTCIS is EGLE's public-facing system for Michigan's drinking water operator certification program, which oversees nearly 5,000 certified operators serving water supplies across the state. The system allows the public to look up certified operators, search by Water Supply Serial Number (WSSN), and find Advisory Board-approved continuing education courses. EGLE's Operator Training and Certification Unit uses the underlying data to process over 1,000 certification renewals annually and administer biannual exams across four license classifications. The modernization effort is rebuilding the system with current technology to meet WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility standards and align with One State, One Brand design guidelines.

clients.name: Michigan Department of Enivronment, Great Lakes, and Energy

features: Public lookup of ~5,000 certified drinking water operators statewide
Search by Water Supply Serial Number (WSSN) and operator name
Advisory Board-approved continuing education course directory
WCAG 2.1 AA compliance and One State, One Brand alignment

name: Burn Permits
description: Michigan's Burn Permits system lets residents check whether open burning is permitted on a given day in their county and township before burning yard waste or debris. The DNR announced the updated system in April 2026, highlighting the replacement of an outdated clickable map with streamlined county and township dropdown menus, a mobile-friendly "Can I burn today?" flow, and improved clarity around the different burn regulations for northern and southern Lower Peninsula regions. I rebuilt the system to meet WCAG 2.1 AA requirements and align it with the One State, One Brand design initiative.
features: Replaced legacy clickable map with accessible county and township dropdowns
Mobile-friendly "Can I burn today?" flow for quick status checks
WCAG 2.1 AA compliance for federal ADA enforcement deadline
Alignment with One State, One Brand visual and UX standards

name: Intentional Conversation Database (ICDB)
description: The Intentional Conversation Database is MDARD's internal HR tool for tracking structured performance conversations between supervisors and employees — capturing check-ins, goal reviews, policy adherence discussions, and pay-period documentation. The legacy system was built on aging infrastructure with mounting maintenance costs. The modernization effort rebuilds it on a more sustainable, lower-cost architecture while updating the experience to align with state UX and accessibility standards, and improving the workflows supervisors rely on throughout the performance review cycle.

clients.name: Michigan Department of Agriculture and Rural Development

features: Tracks structured performance conversations across the review cycle
Updated UX aligned with state accessibility and design standards
Supports supervisor workflows for check-ins, goals, and pay-period documentation

name: Fisheries Information System Hub (FISH)
description: The Fisheries Information System Hub is the Michigan DNR's central platform for managing commercial fisheries data — supporting electronic reporting, harvest verification, and data management across the state's commercial, charter, and wholesale fishing sectors. The DNR has been moving from paper-based to electronic reporting to improve efficiency, accuracy, timeliness, and enforcement capabilities. I supported platform modernization work to improve the usability of these data workflows for both internal DNR staff and the fisheries operators who rely on the system to report activity and meet regulatory requirements.
features: Electronic reporting for commercial, charter, and wholesale fisheries
Harvest verification and real-time data management workflows
Improved usability for DNR staff and regulated fishing operators
Platform modernization supporting enforcement and policy decisions

name: Water Withdrawal Assessment Tool (WWAT)
description: The Water Withdrawal Assessment Tool is the central component of Michigan's Water Withdrawal Assessment Process — the mechanism by which the state fulfills its Great Lakes Compact obligations and regulates large-quantity water withdrawals before installation. EGLE launched an updated version in September 2025 with MiLogin authentication, improved transparency into watershed allocation data, support for site-specific parameters like transmissivity and storativity, and visibility into the same assessment tools used by EGLE staff. I delivered the initial modernization of the system after its transfer from Michigan State University, ensuring continuity of this critical regulatory tool and improving its digital experience.

clients.name: Michigan Department of Environment, Great Lakes, and Energy

features: Central regulatory tool for Great Lakes Compact water withdrawal compliance
Modernization and continuity after transfer from Michigan State University
Improved transparency into watershed allocations and neighboring withdrawals
Support for site-specific assessment parameters and MiLogin authentication

name: Michigan Forest Inventory (MiFI)
description: MiFI is the Michigan DNR's web-based system for managing the state forest — supporting inventory of every acre, prescription of forest treatments, and execution of commercial timber sales. Originally launched in 2015 after a NASCIO award-winning transformation from a paper-based process, the system enables foresters to complete all field work with a mobile device and attribute data for every acre in the state forest, allowing staff to measure 30% more area per site visit and finalize timber sale forecasts two months earlier than before. I contributed platform work to sustain and extend the system's core business functions for the DNR's Forest Resources Division.
features: Manages inventory, treatment planning, and timber sales across Michigan state forest
Mobile-enabled field workflows covering attribute data for every forested acre
Platform improvements supporting 80 DNR foresters measuring 400,000 acres annually
Sustains a NASCIO award-winning system critical to forest conservation operations

name: MiEHDWIS
description: MiEHDWIS is a multi-year initiative to consolidate EGLE's Drinking Water and Environmental Health Division's permitting, licensing, and compliance information systems into a single web-based platform. The system serves as the primary workspace for regulated entities — including public water supplies, campgrounds, public swimming pools, and septage handlers — to submit monitoring results, reports, permit and licensing applications, and other documentation directly to EGLE. It also streamlines communication between EGLE and local health department staff. I delivered platform improvements supporting these regulated-entity workflows and the DWEHD teams responsible for program oversight.

clients.name: Michigan Department of Environment, Great Lakes, and Energy

features: Unified portal for permits, licenses, monitoring results, and compliance documentation
Serves public water supplies, campgrounds, pools, and septage handlers statewide
Submission tracking, review progress visibility, and EGLE staff messaging
Part of a multi-year DWEHD systems consolidation effort

name: Digital Standards
description: The State of Michigan's Digital Standards for Websites and Applications define the baseline requirements all state departments and agencies must meet — covering state-built sites and apps, custom mobile applications, and vendor-supplied software. Version 2.0 adopted WCAG 2.1 Level AA as the state's accessibility standard, with a compliance deadline tied to federal enforcement. The standards are administered by the Department of Technology, Management and Budget and support the broader One State, One Brand initiative to ensure that Michigan's 38 million monthly website users experience consistent, trustworthy interactions across every agency. I contributed experience design and implementation work that informed and aligned with these standards.
features: WCAG 2.1 AA as the statewide accessibility standard across all agencies
Applies to state-built sites, mobile apps, and vendor-supplied software
Supports One State, One Brand consistency for 38M monthly website users
Administered by DTMB as part of Michigan's human-centered digital strategy

name: Digital Guidelines
description: Michigan's Digital Guidelines is the state's official design system — a single source of truth providing a common visual and experiential language for building citizen-facing applications. The system ships WCAG 2.1 AA-compliant components out of the box, along with a Figma design kit for prototyping and a prebuilt component library to accelerate app development. It serves as the implementation layer beneath the Digital Standards, giving agency development teams the concrete patterns, colors, typography, and interaction models they need to build consistent, accessible Michigan.gov experiences. I contributed design and implementation work aligned with the guidelines across multiple agency modernization projects.
features: WCAG 2.1 AA-compliant component library with accessible focus states by default
Figma design kit for prototyping and design-to-dev handoff
Implementation layer for One State, One Brand visual and interaction standards
Used across multiple agency modernization projects to ensure consistency

name: One State, One Brand
description: One State, One Brand is Michigan DTMB's strategic initiative to align branding, experience, and accessibility across all state websites, applications, and digital communications — so that residents, businesses, and visitors encounter a seamless, trustworthy experience no matter which agency they're interacting with. The initiative encompasses human-centered design, legacy technology replacement, expanded MiLogin single sign-on, centralized social media strategy, and statewide digital accessibility standards. It has helped Michigan place in the top five states for overall digital government experience five years running, including fourth in the nation. All of my state agency modernization work is executed within and in service of this initiative.
features: Unified visual identity and UX standards across all Michigan state digital properties
Human-centered design approach serving 38M monthly website users
Encompasses accessibility compliance, MiLogin SSO, and legacy modernization
Michigan ranked top five nationally for digital government experience five years running
